chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

lambda.name: de Broglie wavelength
lambda.note: Wavelength associated with a moving particle; decreases as momentum increases

V.name: accelerating potential
V.note: For an electron accelerated through potential V, KE = eV

locked_facts: λ = h/p = h/(mv) — de Broglie wavelength equals Planck's constant divided by momentum
For an electron accelerated through potential V: λ = 1.226/√V nm (V in volts)
λ = h/√(2mKE) — wavelength in terms of kinetic energy
For electron accelerated through V volts: p = √(2meV) where m = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg, e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C
Davisson-Germer experiment (1927) confirmed electron diffraction, validating de Broglie's hypothesis
Heavier particles have shorter wavelengths at the same speed: λ ∝ 1/m
A cricket ball (0.15 kg at 30 m/s) has λ ≈ 1.5×10⁻³⁴ m — undetectably small, so no observable wave nature
truth_statements: Every moving particle has an associated matter wave — this is universal, not limited to electrons
de Broglie wavelength is significant only for microscopic particles because λ = h/p and h is extremely small (6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s)
The Davisson-Germer experiment showed electron diffraction from nickel crystal at specific angles, exactly matching the predicted de Broglie wavelength
Wave-particle duality means particles exhibit wave behavior (diffraction, interference) and waves exhibit particle behavior (photoelectric effect) — both aspects are real
The de Broglie relation connects mechanics (p = mv) with wave optics (λ) through Planck's constant h

aha_moment.description: Student sees an electron beam passing through a crystal lattice and producing a diffraction pattern identical to X-ray diffraction — proving the electron IS a wave. Then the wavelength calculated from λ = h/p matches the diffraction pattern spacing exactly.
aha_moment.visual_cue: Side-by-side: X-ray diffraction pattern vs electron diffraction pattern from same crystal — they match when λ values are equal

STATE_1.title: Particle with a Wave
STATE_1.description: Show a moving electron as both a particle (dot) and an associated wave (sinusoidal envelope). Display λ = h/(mv). As speed increases, wavelength shrinks visibly. Compare electron wavelength to atomic spacing (~10⁻¹⁰ m).

STATE_2.title: Why Only Small Particles Show Wave Nature
STATE_2.description: Compare de Broglie wavelengths: electron at 10⁶ m/s (λ ≈ 0.7 nm), proton at same speed (λ ≈ 0.4 pm), cricket ball at 30 m/s (λ ≈ 10⁻³⁴ m). Show that macroscopic objects have wavelengths far smaller than any detector can resolve.

STATE_3.title: Davisson-Germer Proof
STATE_3.description: Animate the Davisson-Germer setup: electron gun → nickel crystal → detector at angle θ. Show diffraction peaks appearing at angles predicted by nλ = d sinθ. Compare calculated λ from de Broglie formula with λ from diffraction formula — they match.

STATE_4.title: Interactive Exploration
STATE_4.description: Student adjusts particle type, velocity, and accelerating potential. Real-time calculation of de Broglie wavelength. Visual wave envelope scales with λ. Challenge: find V that gives electron wavelength equal to atomic spacing (≈ 1 Å).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Only electrons have de Broglie wavelength; macroscopic objects don't have wave nature
common_misconceptions.correct: ALL moving objects have a de Broglie wavelength λ = h/(mv). Macroscopic objects have wavelengths so tiny (10⁻³⁴ m) that wave effects are undetectable, but the wavelength still exists.
trigger_phrases: sirf electron ka wave hota hai

common_misconceptions.misconception: Faster particles have longer de Broglie wavelength
common_misconceptions.correct: λ = h/(mv) — wavelength is INVERSELY proportional to velocity. Faster particles have SHORTER wavelengths. This is why high-energy electrons are used in electron microscopes for better resolution.

common_misconceptions.misconception: de Broglie waves are electromagnetic waves like light
common_misconceptions.correct: Matter waves (de Broglie waves) are probability waves — they describe the probability of finding the particle at a given location. They are NOT electromagnetic waves and do not have electric or magnetic field components.
trigger_phrases: matter wave is like light wave
de Broglie wave is EM wave

legend.electron_dot: Blue glowing dot representing the particle aspect of the electron
legend.matter_wave: Sinusoidal envelope around particle showing associated de Broglie wave with wavelength λ
legend.crystal_lattice: Regular array of nickel atoms with spacing d used in diffraction
legend.diffraction_ring: Circular rings showing constructive interference pattern from electron diffraction
legend.wavelength_scale: Ruler comparing λ to atomic spacing (Angstroms) or nuclear size (femtometers)

concept_relationships.relationship: Photoelectric effect proves particle nature of light; de Broglie hypothesis proves wave nature of matter — together they establish wave-particle duality
Bohr's quantization condition L = nℏ is equivalent to requiring an integer number of de Broglie wavelengths to fit in each orbit: 2πr = nλ
Electron diffraction experiments directly measure de Broglie wavelength via Bragg's law
de Broglie wave packets connect to Heisenberg uncertainty: Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2
